era_context: The late 15th century saw the consolidation of early modern European nation-states, Ottoman expansion in the Eastern Mediterranean, Ming Dynasty stability in China, and the final stages of the Reconquista in Iberia. This was a period of dynastic succession, religious conflict, and the early Age of Exploration.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (beyond Portuguese coastal trading posts)

events.title: Ottoman Conquest of Crimea
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Mehmed II, conquered the Crimean Khanate in 1476, bringing the region under Ottoman suzerainty. The Crimean Khan became a vassal of the Ottoman Sultan. This conquest extended Ottoman control over the Black Sea region and eliminated a rival power. The conquest had significant implications for the balance of power in Eastern Europe and the Black Sea, affecting Genoese, Venetian, and Russian interests.
events.certainty_note: The Ottoman conquest of Crimea in 1476 is well-documented in Ottoman sources. The exact date may vary slightly (1475–1476) depending on the source.

events.title: Sixtus IV's Death; Accession of Innocent VIII
events.description: Pope Sixtus IV died on August 12, 1484 (not 1476). However, 1476 saw significant papal politics under Sixtus IV, including his involvement in the Pazzi Conspiracy and his conflicts with Venice and Florence. No major papal succession occurred in 1476.
events.certainty_note: Sixtus IV died in 1484, not 1476. This entry requires correction; no major papal succession event is confirmed for 1476.

events.title: Venetian-Ottoman War Continues
events.description: The Venetian-Ottoman War (1463–1479) continued with Ottoman forces maintaining pressure on Venetian holdings. In 1476, Ottoman naval dominance was evident, and Venice was increasingly forced to negotiate. The war was economically draining for Venice.
events.certainty_note: The war's continuation in 1476 is well-documented.

events.title: Ming Dynasty Stability
events.description: The Chenghua Emperor continued to consolidate Ming Dynasty power. The dynasty was stable, and the emperor was focused on internal administration. This period saw continued economic development in China.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: General Ming Dynasty stability is well-documented; specific events for 1476 are not prominent.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration of African Coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers continued systematic exploration of the West African coast. Portuguese traders were establishing trading posts and acquiring gold, ivory, and enslaved people.
events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration is well-documented; specific events for 1476 are less detailed.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

disconfirming_evidence: The Ottoman conquest of Crimea is sometimes presented as a sudden military victory, but modern scholarship emphasizes the gradual process of Ottoman expansion and the complex negotiations with the Crimean Khan. The conquest did not immediately eliminate Crimean independence; the Khan retained significant autonomy as an Ottoman vassal. The entry on Sixtus IV's death (1476-002) contains a dating error: Sixtus IV died in 1484, not 1476.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1476 is moderate in Western European sources and rich in Ottoman sources. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are available but focus on court politics. African, American, and Asian sources beyond China are sparse. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events in Europe and the Ottoman Empire; economic and social change in Africa and Asia are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Ottoman conquest of Crimea emboldened Mehmed II to launch invasion of Otranto
Continued Ottoman pressure led to Treaty of Constantinople
